coredata.dc:title: Octadecylamine and serine-derived carbon dots-modified silica gel for reversed phase/hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Chenyang
Yan, Shili
Liu, Jun
Xiong, Zhili
Zhao, Longshan
coredata.dc:description: In order to meet the demand for the separation of complex samples, the development of mixed-mode chromatographic stationary phases has become one of the research priorities in chromatographic separation. In this work, carbon dots, Ser-OCDs, with amino, hydroxyl, carboxyl and alkyl chains were synthesized for the first time and modified on silica gel for RPLC/HILIC mixed-mode chromatography. The stationary phase, Sil-Ser-OCDs, enables the effective separation of hydrophobic and hydrophilic compounds such as alkylbenzenes, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, nucleosides, bases and sulfonamides with better separation selectivity and higher column efficiency compared to classical C18 column. Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, nucleosides, bases and sulfonamides can all be separated in less than 10 min, Moreover, the column efficiency (theoretical plates, N) of butylbenzene was even as high as 124,024 plates m−1. By investigating the effects of column temperature and concentration of buffer salt solution on retention and constructing the van't Hoff equation, hydrogen bonding, π-π conjugation, electrostatic repulsion, partitioning and hydrophobic interactions were found to be the main retention interactions. Sil-Ser-OCDs column was eluted for a long time at pH 3 and 8, and the relative retention remained basically unchanged, which proved that the stability of the column was excellent. This work broadens the variety of chromatographic stationary phase materials, at the same time, indicates that carbon dots have promising applications in mixed-mode chromatography.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Silica gel
Stationary phase
Chromatographic analysis
Mixed-mode chromatography

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Chromatographic behavior and application of Sil-Ser-OCDs
Chromatographic behavior in reversed-phase retention mode
Chromatographic behavior in hydrophilic retention mode
Effect of NH4AcO on retention behavior
Effect of column temperature on retention behavior

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram of synthesis process of Sil-Ser-OCDs stationary phase.
TEM of Ser-OCDs (a); Particle diameter distribution of Ser-OCDs (b); XPS spectra of Ser-OCDs (c) and high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (d), N 1s (e).
SEM of silica gel (a) and Sil-Ser-OCDs (b).
FT-IR spectrum of Ser-OCDs and Sil-Ser-OCDs (a); TGA curve of Sil-Ser-OCDs stationary phase (b); Contact angle analysis results of Ser-OCDs, Sil-IPTS, Sil-Ser-OCDs (c).

Chromatographic separation of alkylbenzenes (a), polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (b), nucleosides and bases (c) and sulfonamides (d) on a commercial C18 column (blue) and Sil-Ser-OCDs column (red), respectively. Mobile phase (The former is C18 and the latter is Sil-Ser-OCDs): (a) methanol/water (85/15, v/v), methanol/water (40/60, v/v), (b) acetonitrile/water (80/20, v/v), acetonitrile/water (55/45, v/v), (c) acetonitrile/water (90/10, v/v), acetonitrile/water (93/7, v/v), (d) acetonitrile/10 mM ammonium acetate (10/90 and up to 40/60 within 20 min, v/v), acetonitrile /10 mM ammonium acetate (30/70, v/v). Flow rate: 1 mL/min. Detection wavelength: 254 nm. Analytes: (a) 1. toluene, 2. ethylbenzene, 3. propylbenzene, 4. n-butylbenzene, 5. n-pentylbenzene; (b) 1. naphthalene, 2. fluorene, 3. anthracene, 4. triphenylbenzene; (c) 1. thymine, 2. uridine, 3. adenosine, 4. cytidine, 5. cytosine;(d) 1. sulfadiazine, 2. sulfadiazine, 3. sulfamethazine, 4. Sulfadimethylpyrimidine.
chromatographic separation parameters of alkylbenzenes on Sil-Ser-OCDs column.
Van't Hoff equation and its parameters.

head.ce:title: Octadecylamine and serine-derived carbon dots-modified silica gel for reversed phase/hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Shenyang, Liaoning 110016, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Shenyang, Liaoning 110016, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Zafex Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd, Rushan, Shandong 264599, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Zafex Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shandong Zhefen Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd, Rushan, Shandong 264599, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road Shenhe District, 110016 Shenyang, Liaoning Province, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In order to meet the demand for the separation of complex samples, the development of mixed-mode chromatographic stationary phases has become one of the research priorities in chromatographic separation. In this work, carbon dots, Ser-OCDs, with amino, hydroxyl, carboxyl and alkyl chains were synthesized for the first time and modified on silica gel for RPLC/HILIC mixed-mode chromatography. The stationary phase, Sil-Ser-OCDs, enables the effective separation of hydrophobic and hydrophilic compounds such as alkylbenzenes, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, nucleosides, bases and sulfonamides with better separation selectivity and higher column efficiency compared to classical C18 column. Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, nucleosides, bases and sulfonamides can all be separated in less than 10 min, Moreover, the column efficiency (theoretical plates, N) of butylbenzene was even as high as 124,024 plates m−1. By investigating the effects of column temperature and concentration of buffer salt solution on retention and constructing the van't Hoff equation, hydrogen bonding, π-π conjugation, electrostatic repulsion, partitioning and hydrophobic interactions were found to be the main retention interactions. Sil-Ser-OCDs column was eluted for a long time at pH 3 and 8, and the relative retention remained basically unchanged, which proved that the stability of the column was excellent. This work broadens the variety of chromatographic stationary phase materials, at the same time, indicates that carbon dots have promising applications in mixed-mode chromatography.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), with its high selectivity and sensitivity, has become one of the most widely used separation techniques, playing an indispensable role in fields such as medicine, food, and environmental science [1,2]. The stationary phase is an important component of the HPLC system, and the retention mechanism, separation selectivity and column efficiency depend mainly on the structure and properties of the stationary phase [3]. Currently, according to the different retention mechanisms of liquid chromatography, liquid chromatography stationary phases can be divided into reversed-phase liquid chromatography (RPLC), normal phase chromatography (NPC), hydrophilic-interaction chromatography (HILIC), ion-exchange chromatography (IEC), and size exclusion chromatography (SEC) [4–8], and the columns are selected according to different separation needs. RPLC is the most widely used separation method in HPLC, but the traditional reversed-phase C18 column tends to collapse in the highly aqueous mobile phase and has relatively poor separation performance for polar compounds [9]. And HILIC is also gradually starting to replace NPC for better separation of polar compounds [10]. However, in recent years, with the advancement of separation science research and the increasing complexity of the components in separated samples, liquid chromatography with only a single retention mechanism can no longer meet the needs of people. Therefore, the development of new stationary phases with higher separation selectivity and a wide range of uses has become one of the research priorities in chromatographic analysis.

The concept of mixed-mode chromatography (MMC) stationary phase was first introduced by Mc Laughlin in the 1980s [11] and thus gradually came into the limelight. MMC is a chromatographic method in which multiple modes of interaction exist between the stationary phase and the analytes [12], with high selectivity, high loading capacity and resolution, which offers the possibility to achieve efficient separation of compounds with widely differing chemical properties [13–16]. According to the different separation mechanisms, MMC is mainly divided into RPLC/HILIC [17], RPLC/IEC [18], and HILIC/IEC [19]. At present, the preparation of MMC stationary phases consists of two ways, one is to heterogeneously load stationary phases with different retention mechanisms, but it is rarely used due to poor reproducibility, while the other is to synthesize stationary phases with multiple functional groups on the surface, and the difference in the type and number of functional groups will make them show different retention properties, which is the mainstream preparation method. In addition, the emergence of some new materials such as Covalent organic frameworks (COFs), Metal organic Frameworks (MOFs), graphene and other carbon nanomaterials have received a lot of attention from researchers [20–22]. Zheng [23] et al. synthesized stationary phase covalent organic frameworks (COFs) modified silica SiO2 @COF, which can provide a variety of interaction modes including hydrophobic, hydrophilic, hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions. Jiang [24] et al. bonded hydroxyl-modified multi-walled carbon nanotubes to silica as a chromatographic stationary phase for the separation of organic acids, amines, substituted aromatic compounds and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons. However, since most of these materials are expensive or complicated to prepare, the search for chromatographic materials with simple synthesis, low cost and diverse functional groups has become an urgent need in separation science.

As a new type of carbon-based nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have outstanding features such as small size, simple synthesis, high stability, large surface area and diverse groups, and are now mainly used in biosensing [25], photocatalysis [26] and bioimaging [27]. However, in recent years, some researchers have proposed and argued that CDs can also have good prospects in the field of chromatographic separation [28], and due to the tunability of group species, CDs with different properties can be prepared according to different needs and have certain designability.

It has been shown that if compounds containing hydrophobic groups are used as carbon sources in the synthesis of CDs and appropriate reaction conditions are controlled, it is possible to make carbon dots with hydrophobic properties as well [29]. Herein, Ser-OCDs were synthesized using octadecylamine and serine as carbon sources, with hydrophilic and hydrophobic functional groups such as hydroxyl, amino and carboxyl groups as well as alkyl chains. Then it was bonded with silica gel to successfully prepare Sil-Ser-OCDs, a new chromatographic stationary phase with dual retention mechanism of both HILIC and RPLC, and was successfully used to separate alkylbenzenes, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PHAs), nucleosides, bases and sulfonamides. The effect of column temperature and buffer salt concentration on retention was investigated to further investigate the retention mechanism. Compared with conventional C18 columns, Sil-Ser-OCDs offer better separation selectivity along with high stability and lower synthesis cost.

Silica gel (120 Å, 5 μm) was supplied by INB Planning Co., Ltd. (Osaka, Japan). Octadecylamine (90 %) and (3-isocyanatopropyl) triethoxysilane (IPTS, 95 %) were provided by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China), l-serine was purchased from Bidet Pharmaceutical Technology Co., Ltd (97 %, Shanghai, China). All standard chemicals analyzed by HPLC including sulfonamides, nucleosides, bases, alkylbenzenes, PAHs, etc. were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Deionized water was aquired from Hangzhou Wahaha Foods Co., Ltd (Hangzhou, China).

Chromatographic analysis was carried out by Waters HPLC system, equipped with 2695 separations module and 2487 dual λ absorbance detector. Specific surface area testing (BET) was done on the HD88 fully automated specific surface and porosity analyzer (Micromeritics, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken using a JEM-2100F high-resolution transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). Fourier infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were recorded by a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS20 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer and a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI X-ray photoelectron spectrometer, respectively (Thermo Fisher Scientifific, USA). The contact angle (CA) of the material was measured by an OCA40 contact angle meter (Dataphysics, Germany) and the elemental composition was analyzed with a Vario EL Cube elemental analyzer (EA, Elementar, Germany). The variation of the mass of the substances with temperature was measured by a TGA 5500 thermogravimetric analyzer (TA, USA). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were taken by a supra 55 scanning electron microscope (Zeiss, Germany).

As shown in Fig. 1 a, the CDs were prepared by the solvothermal method. In order to synthesize CDs with hydrophilic and hydrophobic groups, octadecylamine containing long alkyl chains and serine containing abundant groups were chosen as precursors. The experimental procedure was as follows: 4 g of octadecylamine and 4 g of serine were dissolved in 30 mL of ethanol, sonicated for 10 min, and then transferred to 100 mL of sealed Teflon autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 5 h. Afterwards, the mixture was centrifuged at 3500 rpm for 10 min to obtain the supernatant, filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane fifilter, and then the filtrate was dialyzed for 24 h using a dialysis bag (MWCO: 1000 Da) and replaced with deionized water at fixed time intervals for further purification. Finally, the purified carbon dots were freeze-dried at −80 °C for 48 h and stored at 4 °C for subsequent experiments.

In order to bond the carbon dots with silica gel, firstly, 5 g of silica gel was weighed in a 250 mL round bottom flask, then, 100 mL of toluene was added, refluxed for 4 h, filtered, and dried at 120 °C for 4 h. Subsequently, 6 g of IPTS and 100 mL of toluene were added to the de-watered silica gel and mixed well as shown in Fig. 1b and Fig. 1c. Under N2 atmosphere, it was heated to reflux for 24 h, after which it was washed with toluene and DMF, respectively, and dried at 100 °C for 10 h to receive Sil-IPTS. Eventually, 5 g of Sil-IPTS and 3 g of Ser-OCDs were added into 100 mL of toluene and heated under N2 atmosphere. After 5 h, the Sil-Ser-OCDs were washed with toluene, acetone and anhydrous ethanol three times consecutively and dried at 100 °C for 8 h.

3 g Sil-Ser-OCDs was sonicated and dispersed in 30 mL of tetrahydrofuran-pyrrolidone (9:1, v/v), and the slurry was loaded into a stainless steel column (4.6 × 250 mm i.d.) under 40 MPa pressure using methanol as the propulsion solvent.

All chromatographic analyses were performed on a Waters 2695 high-performance liquid chromatography system. The retention time of thiourea was used as dead time and the separation performance of Sil-Ser-OCDs column was evaluated using standards with different properties such as aniline, phenol, theobromine, alkylbenzenes, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, nucleosides, bases and sulfonamides. It was also compared with a commercial Welch Ultimate XB-C18 (4.6 × 250 mm i.d.) column and a Spherisorb Amino (NH2) column (4.6 × 100 mm i.d.). The organic phase of the mobile phase was methanol or acetonitrile, and the aqueous phase was pure water or 10 mM ammonium acetate solution. Then, the concentration of the standard stock solution was 1 mg/mL, which was diluted with acetonitrile and filtered through 0.22 μm organic filter membrane for HPLC analysis.

The morphology and dimensions of Ser-OCDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) as shown in Fig. 2 a and Fig. 2b. Ser-OCDs were sphere-like, well dispersed in aqueous solution, brown in natural light and blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light, with a particle size distribution of 3.2–5.8 nm and an average particle size of 4.3 nm. The structure and elemental composition of Ser-OCDs were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). In the XPS spectra of Ser-OCDs (Fig. 2c), the three characteristic peaks of 283.7, 398.0 and 531.9 eV correspond to C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 2d) can be fitted to two peaks at 284 and 285.1 eV, which are ascribed to the CC and CN/CO bonding energies, respectively [30,31]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2e) can be can be fitted to the peak at 397.9 eV, corresponding to the bond of CN [32]. As shown in Fig. 3 , using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), it was found that the surface of silica gel was smooth, while the reaction with Ser-OCDs made the surface rough and showed some small clusters, which indicated that the silica gel was successfully modified.

The surface functional groups and structures of the Ser-OCDs and Sil-Ser-OCDs were characterized by the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 4 a). For Ser-OCDs, the absorption peaks at 3340 cm−1 and 1670 cm−1 are attributed to the OH and CO stretching vibrations, respectively [33,34]. The peak at 1570 cm−1 corresponds to the deformation vibration of NH, and the absorption bands at 2850/2920 cm−1 belong to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching of the CH bond, respectively. For the infrared spectrogram of Sil-Ser-OCDs, 1100 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of Si-O bond and 2940/2860 cm−1 is still from CH bond. 1680 cm−1 and 3650 cm−1 peaks are attributed to the stretching vibration of CO and NH, respectively, which indicates that Ser-OCDs are modified onto silica gel. Based on the above results, it was demonstrated that stationary phase Sil-Ser-OCDs and Ser-OCDs with functional groups such as alkyl chains, carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino groups were successfully prepared.

As shown in Fig. 4b, the mass loss of the Sil-Ser-OCDs stationary phase was found to be very low below 250 °C by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) testing, which shows the high thermal stability of the Sil-Ser-OCDs. In addition, by measuring the content of elements such as C, H, and N in organic materials, elemental analysis (EA) can reveal changes in the properties of the synthesized material. The results are listed in Table S1. Ser-OCDs contained a significant amount of C elements and the Sil-IPTS contained 5.35 % and 1.48 % of C and N elements, while the stationary phase Sil-Ser-OCDs increased to 7.27 % and 1.52 % of C and N, respectively, presumably the Ser-OCDs had been successfully bonded to the Sil-IPTS. Contact angle experiments (CA) are used to test the wettability of solid particles to water. It is generally considered that when the contact angle is less than 90°, the solid surface appears hydrophilic, and if the contact angle is greater than 90°, it proves to be hydrophobic [35]. As shown in Fig. 4c, the test results showed that the contact angles of Ser-OCDs, Sil-IPTS and Sil-Ser-OCDs were 92.1°, 54.5° and 85.8°, respectively, so that the Sil-Ser-OCDs stationary phase may possess both hydrophilic and hydrophobic properties. The BET data of this Sil-Ser-OCDs are listed in Table S2, and the specific surface area, pore volume and pore size were reduced after the modification of Ser-OCDs onto the silica gel, which indicates that the surface morphology of the silica gel has been changed and that these functionalized groups are not only encapsulated on the surface of the silica gel, but also the nanoscale Ser-OCDs may have entered the porous channels of the silica gel [36]. In summary, it is shown that the stationary phase Sil-Ser-OCDs has been successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chromatographic behavior and application of Sil-Ser-OCDs

ce:section.ce:para: In general, aniline, phenol, toluene and ethylbenzene are standard analytes for assessing silanol activity and hydrophobic selectivity [37]. In a given mobile phase, if aniline precedes phenol in the peak, it indicates a lower silanol activity of the stationary phase [38]. As shown in Fig. S1, although aniline was eluted before phenol, the calculated αpheno/aniline was 1.16 and αethylbenzene/toluene was 1.64, which proved that some silanol groups were not completely covered, but the stationary phase still had strong hydrophobic selectivity due to the presence of alkyl chains.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chromatographic behavior in reversed-phase retention mode
ce:section.ce:para: Hydrophobic compounds, alkylbenzenes and PHAs, were used to investigate the retention mechanism of the column. As shown in Fig. 5 a, five alkylbenzenes (toluene, ethylbenzene, propylbenzene, n-butylbenzene and n-pentylbenzene) with progressively increasing hydrophobicity were effectively separated within 18 min using methanol/water as the mobile phase in the same order of elution as the hydrophobic strength, indicating that the retention between the stationary phase and the target analytes was mainly hydrophobic interaction. Using the same mobile phase, a widely used commercial C18 column was selected for the separation of these compounds. Although the Separation effect was similar between the two columns, the C18 column showed poor peak shape and splitting. Table 1 shows the chromatographic separation parameters of alkylbenzenes on Sil-Ser-OCDs columns, including retention factor (k), selection factor (α), Resolution (Rs), trailing factor (T) and column efficiency (N). In particular, the theoretical plates of butylbenzene was as high as 124,024 plates m−1, which represents the ultra-high column efficiency of Sil-Ser-OCDs column.

In the reversed-phase mode, generally, the retention factor of alkylbenzenes increases exponentially with the extension of the carbon chain [39]. The proportion of methanol in the mobile phase was set 55 %, 60 % and 65 % to investigate the effect of the number of methylene in alkylbenzenes on the logarithm of the retention factor (Log k). As shown in Fig. S2a, Log k increases linearly with the number of methylene groups and the larger the proportion of methanol in the mobile phase, the earlier it is eluted and the smaller the k value, which proves that the Sil-Ser-OCDs column has a typical reversed-phase retention mode [38].

PAHs are another conventional of compounds with high hydrophobicity. Based on this feature, naphthalene, fluorene, anthracene and terphenyl were selected to further investigate the separation selectivity of Sil-Ser-OCDs columns and compared with commercial C18 column. As shown in Fig. 5b, similar to the separation of alkylbenzenes, the Sil-Ser-OCDs column achieved baseline separation of PAHs within 10 min. The peak order of the four compounds was the same as that of the C18 column, the elution speed was faster and the analysis time was shorter under the elution conditions of the low proportion of organic phase. This may be due to the fact that CDs have a highly off-domain conjugation system with π-π conjugation to PAHs. The result suggests that separation mechanism of hydrophobic compounds alkylbenzene and PAHs on Sil-Ser-OCDs columns is in reverse-phase retention mode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chromatographic behavior in hydrophilic retention mode
ce:section.ce:para: Since the carbon dots Ser-OCDs contain not only alkyl chains but also hydrophilic functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups, and the stationary phase is not capped during the synthesis of Sil-Ser-OCDs columns, a part of silanol group will still exist on the silica surface, which gives the possibility that Sil-Ser-OCDs can have both reversed-phase and hydrophilic retention modes. Three compounds, theobromine, adenine and cytosine, were selected to investigate the effect of the content of acetonitrile (10 %–90 %) in the mobile phase on the Log k. As shown in Fig. S2b, the curves were “U” shaped with the lowest value of Log k in the range of 50–60 % of acetonitrile content, which indicated that the stationary phase can achieve the transition between hydrophilic and hydrophobic interactions, verifying the correctness of the conjecture that the stationary phase also had HILIC retention mode.

The ratio of cytosine to uracil retention factor, kcytosine/kuracil, has been used as a criterion to assess the strength of hydrophilicity of the stationary phase, and a larger ratio proves that the stationary phase is more hydrophilic [40]. The results of the comparison between commercial HILIC column and Sil-Ser-OCDs column (Fig. S2c) showed that the kcytosine/kuracil values of both columns increased gradually with the increase of acetonitrile content in the mobile phase, but it was obvious that the commercial HILIC column was more hydrophilic than the Sil-Ser-OCDs. Thus, the main investigation of this study was whether the Sil-Ser-OCDs column expanded its application compared to the conventional C18 column.

Five typical hydrophilic nucleosides and bases were selected as target analytes to investigate the hydrophilicity of Sil-Ser-OCDs, as shown in Fig. 5c. Under the chromatographic conditions with a high proportion of acetonitrile as the mobile phase, these strongly polar compounds could achieve baseline separation within 10 min with good peak shape, while they were largely unretentive on the commercial C18 column, which may be attributed to the synergistic effect of carboxyl, hydroxyl, amino and alkyl chains in Sil-Ser-OCDs. In addition, the effect of the ratio of acetonitrile in the mobile phase on the Log k values of the five compounds was studied (Fig. S2d), and it was found that the Log k increased linearly with increasing the ratio of acetonitrile, which was a typical HILIC retention mode, demonstrating the good hydrophilic selectivity of the Sil-Ser-OCDs column. Then, the separation performance of the Sil-Ser-OCDs column was investigated again using four common sulfonamide antibiotics, sulfadiazine, sulfamethazine and sulfadimethazine (Fig. 5d) as examples. Complete separation was achieved within 5 min. In contrast, the C18 column showed problems such as pre-delay and tailing, and the elution time was longer. Therefore, the Sil-Ser-OCDs column showed good separation performance for both Hydrophobic and hydrophilic compounds, which was mainly due to the synergistic interaction between multiple functional groups in the Ser-OCDs, resulting in a multifaceted retention mechanism of the Sil-Ser-OCDs column, including hydrogen bonding, π-π conjugation and hydrophobic interactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of NH4AcO on retention behavior
ce:section.ce:para: Studying the effect of buffer salt concentration in the mobile phase on retention can provide further insight into the retention mechanism of the stationary phase. Four sulfonamides were selected as target analytes to investigate the effect of ammonium acetate buffer concentrations (30 mM, 40 mM, 50 mM, 60 mM, 70 mM, respectively, pH = 6.5) on their retention, as measured by Log k. As shown in Fig. S3, the retention effect of all four compounds increases with increasing concentration of the NH4AcO in the mobile phase. There is an ionic interaction between the ionized groups in the sulfonamides and the Sil-Ser-OCDs, which can be attenuated by the aggregation of the ammonium acetate [41], thus indicating that electrostatic repulsion dominates between them. Moreover, the largest change in Log k among the four compounds was sulforaphane (Fig. S4a), probably due to the stronger repulsion between the negatively charged sulforaphane and the hydroxyl group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of column temperature on retention behavior
ce:section.ce:para: Column temperature also has an important effect on retention, and we examined the changes in retention times of thymine, thymidine, adenosine, and adenine at column temperatures of 25 °C, 30 °C, 35 °C, 40 °C, 45 °C, and 50 °C (Fig. S5), and explored the mechanism using the van't Hoff equation [42].

The van't Hoff equation is as follows:
ln k = -
Δ H
RT
+
Δ S
R
+ ln Φ
where k is the retention factor of the analyte, R represents the gas coefficient, T is absolute temperature, ΔH and ΔS are the enthalpy and entropy changes during the transfer of the analyte from the mobile phase to the stationary phase, respectively, and Φ represents phase ratio, which is a constant value for a particular column and mobile phase.

As shown in Fig. S4b, since R and Φ are constant, ln k is linear with temperature (1/T), which proves that the enthalpy change (ΔH) and entropy change (ΔS) values remain constant and the retention mechanism is not altered by the temperature change. Meanwhile, as shown in Table 2 , the correlation coefficients r2 were all greater than 0.98 and ΔH was negative for all four nucleosides and bases, which proved that the process of the analytes from the mobile phase to the stationary phase was exothermic and the retention time was shortened with the increase of the column temperature. In addition, it has been shown that if ΔHθ is positive, it indicates that the retention mechanism is adsorption; ΔHθ is negative, which means that it is partitioning [43]. Obviously, ΔHθ is negative for all four analytes. As a result, the retention mechanism is partitioning.

To evaluate the column stability, the column was continuously eluted for a long time under extreme pH and high column temperature conditions. The pH of the mobile phase methanol/10 mM ammonium acetate (40/60, v/v) was adjusted to 3 and 8, and the column temperature was 45 °C. Three compounds, sulfacetamide (pKa < 7, at 25 °C), sulfathiazole (pKa = 7, at 25 °C) and betamethasone (pKa > 7, at 25 °C), were selected as analytes, and the samples were injected every half an hour for 20 h. At each pH, two identical batches of columns were selected for testing and the average of the retention times was used as the retention time for each analyte. As shown in Fig. S6a and Fig. S6b, the retention of all three analytes did not fluctuate significantly under prolonged elution of the mobile phase at each pH.

In addition, the repeatability was tested using PHAs (naphthalene, fluorene, anthracene and terphenylene) as analytes with 10 consecutive injections, as shown in Fig. S6c. The RSDS of the retention time were 0.166 %, 0.171 %, 0.187 % and 0.210 %, respectively, and the RSDS of the peak area were 0.716 %, 0.592 %, 0.693 % and 0.789 %, respectively.
In summary, Sil-Ser-OCDs columns can meet the needs of daily analysis with excellent stability.

Ser-OCDs, novel carbon dots with hydroxyl, amino, carboxyl and alkyl chains were synthesized by a solvothermal method using octadecylamine and serine as carbon sources, and then were bonded to silica gel to successfully prepare stationary phases Sil-Ser-OCDs for mixed-mode chromatography. The column has high column efficiency and selectivity for alkylbenzenes and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons in RPLC mode, and can achieve effective separation of hydrophilic compounds including nucleosides, bases and sulfonamides in HILIC mode, which makes up for the poor retention of hydrophilic compounds in conventional C18 columns. By examining the effect of chromatographic conditions on the retention behavior of the analytes, it was found that the stationary phase may have multiple retention mechanisms including hydrogen bonding, π-π conjugation, electrostatic repulsion, partitioning, and hydrophobic interactions. This work enriches the variety of stationary phases for RPLC/HILIC mixed-mode chromatography and can have a good application prospect.

Chenyang Zhao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Shili Yan: Visualization, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Jun Liu: Data curation, Investigation. Zhili Xiong: Software, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Longshan Zhao: Visualization, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support by Scientific Research Fund of Liaoning Provincial Education Department (2020LZD02), and the Project of Shenyang Key Laboratory of Functional Drug Carrier Materials (Grant No. 19-110-4-08).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107987.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
